title: Why the ResNet Family Became a Default
concept: ResNets became a practical default backbone because they paired a clear residual design with multiple depth options, giving later vision systems a dependable way to reuse strong CNN features without reinventing the core network each time.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What made the ResNet family flexible enough to appear in many later systems?

options.text: It offered a reusable residual backbone idea in several depth and cost levels
It only worked for one exact 50-layer setting
It removed the need for any downstream task-specific heads

exercises.explain: Right — the family was practical because it was reusable, scalable, and understandable.

cards.title: The backbone people could reach for first
cards.body: A lot of later vision work did not start from scratch. Teams often wanted a dependable feature extractor they already understood. ResNet filled that role: not mysterious, not fragile, and available in several sizes.

cards.title: What 'default backbone' means
cards.body: Calling ResNet a default backbone means many systems reused its feature-extraction stages as the front half of a larger model. A classifier, detector, or segmentation system could plug in a ResNet backbone, then attach a task-specific head on top of those learned visual features.

cards.title: A reliable frame under many buildings
cards.body: Think of a sturdy steel frame that architects trust for offices, apartments, and labs. The final buildings differ, but the frame is dependable enough to reuse. ResNet played a similar role for vision: different heads, one trusted backbone family.

cards.title: Why practitioners liked it
cards.body: ResNet gave practitioners a clean menu: choose a smaller or larger residual backbone, keep the strong convolutional feature hierarchy, and connect it to the task at hand. That made it natural for transfer learning and for larger systems that needed a proven visual front end.

cards.title: Default does not mean permanent champion
cards.body: Being a default is not the same as being the best choice forever. It means a family became a trusted starting point because the tradeoffs were understandable and the features were useful. Later architectures could beat it on some tasks while still being compared against it.

cards.title: Why the family traveled well
cards.body: ResNet spread because it combined clarity, scalability, and reuse.

left.text:
Residual design was easy to reason about
Multiple depth options fit different budgets
Useful backbone for many vision tasks

right.text:
A reusable feature extractor
Strong pretrained visual stages
Less need to invent a new CNN core every time

exercises.prompt: Why is it fair to call ResNet a common default backbone rather than just a famous classifier?

options.text: Because many later systems reused its residual feature-extraction stages and attached their own task-specific heads
Because ResNet made downstream heads impossible
Because only one exact ResNet variant could ever be pretrained
Because the family stopped being a CNN and became a transformer

exercises.explain: Exactly — the backbone idea is about reusable visual stages, not just one end-to-end classifier.

exercises.prompt: True or false: ResNet became widely reused partly because the family offered several understandable depth choices under one shared residual design pattern.
exercises.explain: True — that menu of related backbones made adoption easier.

exercises.sentence: A detector or segmenter can reuse a ResNet ___ and add a task-specific head on top.
exercises.explain: Right — the backbone is the reusable feature extractor beneath the task head.

exercises.prompt: Which statement best explains why the ResNet family became a practical default?

options.text: It combined a clear residual recipe with multiple scalable depth options, so many systems could reuse it as a trusted feature extractor
It mattered only because it had bigger model numbers than VGG
It became default because residual shortcuts remove the need for any later architecture ideas

exercises.explain: Yes — the default status came from reuse value, not from magic.

exercises.prompt: If ResNet became the default family for reusing visual features, what is the most natural next question for the next architecture family to ask?

options.text: If residual addition already helps feature reuse, could a new family pass features forward even more explicitly between blocks — which is exactly the bridge into DenseNet
Whether CNNs should stop using feature maps and learn directly from image filenames
Whether every future model must copy ResNet exactly forever
Whether skip connections mean no other information-flow design is worth studying

exercises.explain: Exactly. ResNet made feature reuse through residual addition feel normal. DenseNet keeps the same broad ambition — preserving and reusing useful information through depth — but explores a different wiring idea: instead of mostly adding updates back in, what if later layers receive earlier features more directly through dense connectivity? Next up: DenseNet.
